## PharmaSynth Manuscript — Chapter 2 (Review of Related Literature), bytes ~26,000–47,000

This chunk is pure literature review (low design value, as flagged). It motivates the project and cites prior VR-lab / pedagogy research, but does contain a few concrete statements that constrain PharmaSynth's design and a couple of named game mechanics. Sections covered: (1) resource shortages in pharmacy education, (2) VR in Education & Training, (3) Pharmaceutical Synthesis & Medicinal Chemistry, (4) Gamification & Interactive Learning, (5) Bayesian Algorithm, (6) start of Conceptual Framework (cut off).

### 1. Problem motivation (why PharmaSynth exists)
- Pandemic-era campus closures, remote compounding sessions, and global shortages of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and excipients restricted hands-on pharmacy lab training; online sessions couldn't replace experiential face-to-face labs (Alford & Shrewsbury 2021; Ayati et al. 2021; Morling et al. 2022).
- **PharmaSynth's stated purpose:** an interactive virtual lab simulation where students engage with essential **synthesis protocols without real materials**, replicating real-world conditions in a controlled virtual space so students can **observe chemical reactions, navigate/move equipment around the lab, and repeat experiments safely** — reinforcing **procedural awareness and spatial reasoning**. (These are direct design constraints: room-scale lab navigation, grabbable/movable equipment, repeatable experiment runs.)

### 2. VR in Education & Training (evidence base + implied features)
- Cited benefits of VR: improved memory recall, procedural-skill mastery, and engagement; interaction with **3D depictions of molecules, drug mechanisms, and biological processes** makes abstract ideas tangible (Bavelier et al. 2021).
- Li et al. (2021), Hong Kong pilot: VR cardiac-drug module built with **Kern's six-step curriculum design process**; n=32 medical students; pre/post tests; **90% participant satisfaction**; 3D visualization better at explaining intricate drug mechanisms. (Suggests a validated instructional-design model and a pre-test/post-test assessment framing.)
- Sung et al. (2024): systematic review + meta-analysis of **45 randomized controlled trials** — VR significantly improved health students' knowledge, skills, and attitudes.
- Implied features reiterated: controlled, simulated environments to practice drug injection/patient care without real-world consequences; risk-free experimentation.

### 3. Pharmaceutical Synthesis & Medicinal Chemistry (domain the game teaches)
- Synthetic pharmacy = synthesizing/optimizing novel chemical compounds as drugs; limited by chemical complexity, many-step processes, severe conditions, high cost/waste, and **unpredictable reaction outcomes (trial-and-error)**.
- AI retrosynthetic analysis can predict best synthetic routes and reduce trial-and-error (Johnson et al. 2021).
- VR value: immersive interaction with **interactive 3D molecular models** to see how structures fit together in biological reactions; simulate lab experiments safely/cheaply; **experiment with competing chemical reactions, alter conditions, and observe consequences in real time** (Chang et al. 2025; Sleight et al. 2021).
- PharmaSynth stated goal: combine AI + VR to **simulate synthesis processes** so students practice and refine complex procedures without real lab facilities.

### 4. Gamification & Interactive Learning (concrete mechanics)
- Evidence: gamification boosts intrinsic motivation/engagement (Deterding et al. 2021); interactive learning improves comprehension/recall (Sleight et al. 2021); gamified+interactive environments improved test performance and material understanding vs. non-gamified (Delage et al. 2024).
- **Design-relevant mechanics named for PharmaSynth:**
  - Build **3D molecular models and synthesis routes**; **interactively change virtual molecules in real time** and observe how alterations affect the final synthesis product.
  - **Level/challenge progression:** students unlock new challenges/levels based on capability; completing challenges/missions advances them.
  - **Immediate feedback loop:** quick feedback after every challenge, with the chance to **redo bad moves at once** (instant retry / error-correction).
  - Emphasis on developing **critical thinking and problem-solving** in compounding procedures like synthesis and drug fabrication.

### 5. Bayesian Algorithm (a named PharmaSynth system component)
- Broad pharmacology uses cited (ADE detection, precision dosing, DDI prediction, virtual trials, drug-combination prediction) — mostly background.
- **Direct design statement:** In PharmaSynth, Bayesian algorithms are used to **model optimal synthesis routes, predict reaction outcomes, and help learners reason about experimental decisions under uncertainty.** Students **create models for several conditions, receive probabilistic feedback, and explore alternative synthesis routes.** Intended to familiarize students with modern decision-support/computational tools. (This is the scoring/feedback-engine concept: a probabilistic model that rates a chosen synthesis path and gives likelihood-based feedback rather than binary right/wrong.)

### 6. Conceptual Framework
- Section begins at the very end of the chunk: "Figure 1 outlines the evolution of Pharma…" (content is in the next chunk — bytes >47,000). Flag Figure 1 for the follow-up read.

### Design takeaways for the Unity rebuild
- Core loop implied: enter virtual lab → navigate/pick up movable equipment → build/manipulate 3D molecules → run a synthesis with adjustable conditions → observe reaction in real time → get probabilistic (Bayesian) feedback → retry immediately or advance to next level.
- Assessment scaffolding hinted: pre-test/post-test model; Kern six-step instructional design; level-gated challenges tied to demonstrated capability.
- Feedback system is probabilistic (Bayesian), not strictly pass/fail — supports partial-credit / likelihood scoring and multiple valid synthesis routes.
- No specific experiment procedures, named drugs, scenes, UI layouts, NPCs, or asset lists appear in this chunk — those must come from later (design/methodology) chapters.
key_facts: PharmaSynth is a VR virtual lab where students perform pharmaceutical SYNTHESIS protocols without real materials: observe chemical reactions, navigate/move lab equipment, and repeat experiments safely (reinforcing procedural awareness + spatial reasoning).
Named game mechanic — level/challenge progression: challenges/levels unlock based on student capability; completing missions advances the player.
Named game mechanic — immediate feedback loop: quick feedback after each challenge with the ability to redo bad moves instantly (instant retry / error correction).

Named game mechanic — interactive 3D molecular model building: students build/edit molecules and synthesis routes in real time and see how changes affect the final synthesis product; adjust reaction conditions and watch outcomes in real time.

Bayesian algorithm is a core PharmaSynth component: models optimal synthesis routes, predicts reaction outcomes, and gives PROBABILISTIC feedback on experimental decisions under uncertainty (implies likelihood-based scoring and multiple valid routes rather than binary pass/fail).

Instructional-design references that may shape assessment: Kern's six-step curriculum process and pre-test/post-test evaluation (Li et al. 2021, 90% satisfaction, n=32); VR efficacy backed by meta-analysis of 45 RCTs (Sung et al. 2024).

## PharmaSynth Manuscript Digest — Bytes ~46900–71300 (End of Ch.2 + Ch.3 Project Design)

This span covers the tail of **Chapter 2** (Conceptual Framework, System Architecture, Definition of Terms) and most of **Chapter 3 Research Methodology / Project Design** (design narrative, three lobby designs, constraints table, trade-off math, and the chosen design). Chunk ends mid-sentence in the "Project Development" section (Figure 13).

---

### CHAPTER 2 (tail)

**Conceptual Framework (Figure 1)**
- Development methodology: **Rapid Application Development (RAD)** paradigm.
- Flow: requirement planning & user-needs analysis → simple/intuitive **interface design** → building the actual **VR simulations** (focused on synthesis procedures) → **testing and release**.
- Key inputs feeding the system: domain expertise in **medicinal chemistry**, **VR environments**, **3D modeling**, and technical tools — explicitly **Unity, Blender, and VR hardware**.
- Purpose framed as overcoming shortcomings of (a) classic lab-based learning (safety hazards, limited equipment/reagent access) and (b) existing digital chemistry tools (only molecular visualization or restrictive drag-and-drop).
- Output: immersive VR sim enabling personalized learning, **adaptive feedback via Bayesian algorithms**, and alignment to pharmaceutical curricula.
- Quality standard: product tested against **ISO/IEC 25010** criteria — emphasis on **functionality, efficiency, user interaction (usability), and performance**.

**System Architecture (Figure 2) — three-tier pattern:**
1. **Presentation Tier** — Learners interact with the sim via **VR hardware**; this is the interface to the modules: **Tutorials, Lab Experiments (Prelim, Midterm, Final), and System Settings**. Provides natural/intuitive simulation of pharmaceutical procedures.
2. **Logic Tier** — Core system function. The **Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT) Algorithm** monitors user actions and assesses performance **procedure by procedure**. It **dynamically re-orients the training path** and delivers feedback based on student action and progress across modules.
3. **Data Tier** — Handles **asset rendering and simulation execution** to provide real-time visual/interactive responses (described as "minimalistic data handling").
- Design goal of the layering: unconstrained user↔VR interaction + strong logic processing + lightweight data handling → high-fidelity, individualized learning.
- (Note: Figure 2 caption is mislabeled in the doc as "The Conceptual Framework for PharmaSynth," duplicating Figure 1's caption.)

**Definition of Terms (relevant ones):**
- **AI** — used in PharmaSynth to analyze data and suggest efficient synthetic pathways.
- **Bayesian Algorithm** — statistical algorithm based on **Bayes' theorem** for prediction/decision-making; "applied in PharmaSynth to **predict optimal synthesis pathways**."
- **Gamification** — game design elements (scoring, rewards) to boost engagement/learning.
- Also defined: Medicinal Chemistry, Pharmaceutical Synthesis, Pharmacogenomics, Virtual Reality, VR Simulation, Synthetic Pathway.

---

### CHAPTER 3 — Research Methodology / Project Design

**Methodology:** Uses a **developmental research process** (designing, developing, and assessing the system).

**Project Design (core narrative):**
- Curriculum-based, laboratory-like VR environment to compensate for lack of hands-on lab access (equipment inadequacies), letting students perform chemical reactions and explore mechanisms **repeatedly and safely**.
- Development begins with **curriculum review + consultation with medicinal chemistry lecturers** to identify experiments students commonly get wrong/do poorly.
- **Core modules (progressively harder):** **Prelim, Midterm, Finals.**
  - **Prelim** example experiments: **ethyl alcohol synthesis and aspirin synthesis** (elementary organic synthesis).
- Each experiment built on **Unity 3D**, deployed on **VR-compatible platforms**.
- Interaction: **VR gloves/controllers**, **voice-free instructions**, immersive 3D; simulates real lab equipment, safety procedures, and step-by-step synthesis protocols.

**Scoring / Bayesian mastery model:**
- Bayesian algorithm evaluates user performance; scoring is **mastery-based**, judged on: **correct sequences, timeliness, safety-protocol adherence, and successful synthesis outcomes.**
- **Gate: users must reach ≥90% mastery** on an experiment before unlocking the next one.

**Development scope explicitly listed:**
- VR modeling of compounds & equipment via **Blender and Unity**.
- **Dynamic reaction simulation**: color change, heat effects, and reaction yield.
- Performance-based progression via **Bayesian mastery models**.
- **Avatar reflection feedback** — users see themselves wearing safety equipment (immersion + safety awareness).
- Iterative usability & educational-efficacy trials with faculty and pharmacy students refine interaction mechanics, scoring functions, visualizations.

**Design Discussion (grounded in experiential learning theory):**
- Modular VR lab: **tutorial, prelim, midterm, final** modules mirroring rising complexity / academic progression.
- Users manipulate **digital reagents and equipment**, run guided synthesis protocols, visualize real-time chemical processes; supported by an **in-built AI virtual assistant** for feedback.
- UX: **hand-tracking input controls, context-sensing tooltips, navigation cues** mimicking real lab processes.
- **Error handling:** instant **visual AND auditory feedback** whenever mistakes are made — turning errors into learning without physical harm.
- **Timer + progress bar** replicate real lab evaluation (precision, time management).
- **Accessibility:** adjustable audio settings, voice-over instructions, customizable pacing for different familiarity levels.

---

### THE THREE CANDIDATE LOBBY DESIGNS

- **Design 1 (Figure 3):** Futuristic, high-immersion — **neon blue-lit lab interior**, molecular-structure visuals, **holographic buttons** (Tutorial, Laboratory, Settings, Quit) centered. Minimalist. Most immersive; heaviest on resources (neon lighting, holograms, emissive materials, detailed textures, post-processing).
- **Design 2 (Figure 4):** Clean, realistic, professional **academic pharmaceutical lab** — neutral background, a defined **door into the virtual lab**, prominent clearly-labeled buttons (Tutorial, Laboratory, Settings, Quit). Emphasis on familiarity, utility, low cognitive load. Static objects, subdued textures, static/baked lighting.
- **Design 3 (Figure 5):** Minimalist modern **tidy pharmacy lab** — soft white color scheme, **wooden cabinets**, gentle lighting, **floating/suspended UI buttons**, floating molecules, plus lab props (microscopes, glassware). Calm, accessible; moderate resource use (decorative wood textures, ambient lighting, floating molecular models).

All three share the same four buttons: **Tutorial, Laboratory, Settings, Quit.**

---

### CONSTRAINTS TABLE (Table 1) — the authoritative numbers used in the trade-off math

| Design | File/Build Size | Memory Usage | Render Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design 1 (neon/holographic) | **212 MB** | **623 MB** | **13.2 ms/frame** |
| Design 2 (realistic academic lab) | **128 MB** | **472 MB** | **11.8 ms/frame** |
| Design 3 (minimalist soft/wood) | **144 MB** | **534 MB** | **12.6 ms/frame** |

**IMPORTANT DATA INCONSISTENCY:** The prose paragraph before Table 1 states different figures — it claims Design 1 has "best frame responsiveness at **11.8 ms**" and Design 2 the "lowest render time at **11.2 ms**." The **Table 1 values (D1=13.2, D2=11.8) are the ones actually used** in every downstream trade-off computation, so treat the table as canonical and the prose render-time claims as an error.

---

### TRADE-OFF METHOD & COMPUTATIONS

**Formulas (as written; note the second is garbled but the worked examples reveal true intent):**
- Eq. 1 — `% Difference for MIN = (higher value − lower value) / higher value`
- Eq. 2 (as printed): `Subordinate Rank = Governing Rank − (% Difference) − 10` — but **all worked examples actually use** `Subordinate Rank = 10 − [(% Difference) × 10]`.
- **0–10 priority scale** (Figure 6): 0 = lowest priority, 10 = top/best. The design meeting a criterion best scores 10; others scored by % difference from the top.
- **Design 2 is the reference "10" (best) in every criterion.**

**Trade-off 1 — File Size (Table 2):**
- D2 vs D1: %diff = 0.396 → D1 rank = **6.03** (D2 128 MB vs D1 212 MB; neon/holo/textures inflate D1).
- D2 vs D3: %diff = 0.11 → D3 rank = **8.89** (D3 144 MB; wood cabinets, floating molecules, ambient light).

**Trade-off 2 — Memory Usage (Table 3):**
- D2 vs D1: %diff = 0.24 → D1 rank = **7.56** (D1 dynamic neon lighting, emissive, holographic UI → high VRAM).
- D2 vs D3: %diff = 0.12 → D3 rank = **8.8**.

**Trade-off 3 — Render Time (Table 4):**
- D2 vs D1: %diff = 0.11 → D1 rank = **8.9** (D1 13.2 ms vs D2 11.8 ms).
- D2 vs D3: %diff = 0.06 (text then writes 0.12 in the formula line — another minor arithmetic slip) → D3 rank = **9.4** (D3 12.6 ms; ambient lighting + mesh detail of floating molecules + wood materials).

Figures 7–12 are per-criterion "Performance Ranking Scale" charts for Designs 1 and 3.

---

### CHOSEN DESIGN

**Design 2 was selected as the final PharmaSynth interface.** Rationale:
- Best **balance of realism, performance, and usability**.
- **Best on all three measured metrics** in Table 1: lowest memory (472 MB), smallest build (128 MB), fastest render (11.8 ms/frame).
- Realistic university-lab look with clear navigation and grounded visual structure; professional appearance without taxing system resources.
- Explicit conscious trade-off of visual flashiness (Design 1's immersion) for **technical performance + accessibility + frame stability on mid-range VR hardware**, prioritizing legibility, usability, and alignment with educational learning objectives.
- Design 1 = most immersive but heaviest (VRAM, render time). Design 3 = simplest/clearest but "loses immersive depth."

Selection criteria weighed: **render performance, memory usage, user navigability, educational immersion.**

---

### HARDWARE / ENGINE NOTES (as stated in this text vs. task framing)
- The manuscript text in this chunk says only: **Unity 3D** (engine), **Blender** (modeling), **VR gloves/controllers**, "VR-compatible platforms," and targets **mid-range VR systems / common VR platforms** for wide deployment.
- The task's engine stack (Meta Quest, Unity 6, URP, OpenXR + XR Interaction Toolkit) is **NOT explicitly confirmed in this byte range** — the doc here is engine-generic ("Unity 3D," "VR hardware"). Those specifics come from the rebuild brief, not this text.

## PharmaSynth Manuscript Digest — Bytes ~71100–99100 (Ch3 Project Development → start of Appendix C)

This range is the back half of the capstone: development methodology, testing/evaluation, work plan, the (skimmable) commercialization/market/roadmap block, and the appendices. **The single most build-relevant discovery is Appendix B (the per-experiment scoring rubric)** and the explicit RAD/construction details. Note the docs reference an **older tech stack (Unity 3D + Oculus VR SDK)** than the rebuild target (Unity 6 / URP / OpenXR + XRI), and contain a probable template leftover ("Mobile Application Launch") that conflicts with the VR framing.

---

### Ch3 — Project Development (RAD methodology, Figure 13)
The project follows **Rapid Application Development (RAD)**: iterative prototyping, user-centered design, continuous stakeholder feedback. Four phases:

1. **Requirements Planning Phase** — Collaborated with pharmacy professors, medicinal chemists, instructional designers. Core system requirements identified:
   - Realistic compound synthesis of **aspirin, benzoic acid, and ethyl alcohol (ethanol)**.
   - Hands-on reaction simulation.
   - Integration with **CHED's** pharmacy curriculum (CHED = Commission on Higher Education, Philippines).
   - Explicit goal: convert conventional-lab pain points (limited equipment, safety hazards, inconsistent procedural instruction) into **formalized virtual modules with inherent evaluation logic**.

2. **User Design Phase** — Low- and high-fidelity mockups of the VR UI / in-lab experience. Prototyped features: **virtual hand gestures, tool selection, lab safety steps, reaction procedures.** Weekly usability feedback sessions with students + faculty. **Modular structure = Prelim / Midterm / Finals**; learners **unlock later experiments only after mastering earlier modules.** Key NPC design decision: **the first module deliberately has NO bot (solo procedural learning); later modules add AI-supported instructions.**

3. **Construction Phase** — Built in **Unity 3D + Oculus VR SDK**. Chemical reactions simulated via **real-time physics engines + scripted molecular behavior**. **Bayesian algorithm drives the scoring system**, enforcing a **minimum 90% mastery before advancing to the next experiment.** Each module includes safety features: **error detection, simulation resets, and audio-visual feedback that "normalize procedural errors."** 3D models of lab instruments, chemicals, and molecular structures were modeled on **real pharma lab equipment** and optimized for VR.

4. **Cutover Phase** — Deploy to a controlled academic environment for usability testing + classroom pilot. Train students/instructors; collect guided feedback on performance, engagement, comprehension. Use early usage stats to **tune scoring thresholds, tune response times, remove UI bugs.** Stand up a **maintenance pipeline** for content updates, new compound additions, and curriculum scalability.

### Testing and Operating Procedure
- **Internal testing (dev team):** VR simulation rendering, chemical-reaction animation, UI responsiveness, scoring-algorithm performance, curriculum mapping. Module range spans **"minimum synthesis of ethyl alcohol" up to complex experiments.**
- **User Acceptance Testing (UAT):** Instructors validate instructional design, the AI-supported feedback system, and alignment of each module to learning outcomes. Students perform assigned synthesis exercises in VR gear (navigating equipment, receiving Bayesian scoring feedback).
- **Usability testing focus:** motion-control precision, system responsiveness, clarity of virtual instructions, usability of visual cues and reaction outcomes.
- **Feedback capture:** post-session surveys + observation records (ease of use, engagement, system stability).
- **Operational deployment:** university computer labs / VR-capable classrooms; instructors monitor via an **analytics dashboard** for engagement + performance data.

### Project Evaluation — ISO/IEC 25010 (Figure 14), five characteristics
- **Functional Suitability:** must comprehensively and accurately cover aspirin, ethanol, benzoic acid synthesis, contextually appropriate for pharmacy ed.
- **Performance Efficiency:** real-time simulation, efficient resource use, must run in **hardware-limited environments.**
- **Usability:** simple, learnable interface; multi-user operability; **error prevention via guided interaction**; aesthetically professional VR lab to maximize engagement.
- **Reliability:** tolerance for latent errors/crashes without impairing learning; **recovery techniques so users can duplicate/redo their work.**
- **Maintainability:** ease of modification, testing, and expansion — new synthesis modules, future curriculum updates, predictable behavior.

### Work Plan (Table 5)
Four phases; text claims a **Mar–Nov 2025** timeline, but the table columns run **Jun 2025 → Apr 2026** (internal inconsistency). Legend: **Done / On-going / To-do** (green = finished). Tasks by phase:
- **1. Requirements Planning:** Topic/Project Proposal; Documentation of Chapters 1–3; Approval of Project; Hardware Requirements; Development Tools; Consultation with Partner Organization.
- **2. User Design:** Build System Architecture; Build Design and Model; Capstone 1 Defense.
- **3. Development:** Application Development; Alpha and Beta Testing.
- **4. Cutover:** **"Mobile Application Launch"** (likely template artifact — conflicts with the VR/Quest product); Evaluation.

### Commercialization / Market / Roadmap (skim block)
- **Target buyers:** CHED-accredited colleges of pharmacy, universities, health-sciences colleges (esp. resource-limited ones).
- **Market model (Figure 16):** Metro Manila ~15.23M pop (2025); ~990k higher-ed students (6.5%); ~198k in lab-based health-science courses (20%); ~40% at VR-enabled institutions → **SAM ≈ 79,200 students; SOM (10% launch capture) ≈ 7,920 students.**
- **Deployment:** compatible with **VR-capable desktop PCs and standalone headsets**; minimal physical infrastructure; classroom / lab-sim / remote-hybrid friendly.
- **Revenue:** institutional license agreements, student/board-exam-reviewer subscriptions, partnerships with pharmacy associations/government. **Privacy-lean: handles no identifiable data** (low compliance burden).
- **3-Year Roadmap (Figure 17):**
  - **Year 1 (2025):** proof-of-concept; **Unity** VR lab modules for fundamental drug-synthesis pathways; **virtual hand manipulation + sequence-based lab procedures**; pilot tests; **WCC-aligned** (World Citi Colleges) instructional content + safety-protocol simulation.
  - **Year 2 (2026):** higher-level synthesis modules; improved VR responsiveness; **LMS integration**; AI features for educational guidance (esp. controlling reaction mechanisms); **Bayesian grading**; **adaptive difficulty** by performance; interface personalization, **language translations**, editable simulation labs.
  - **Year 3 (2027):** intelligent scalable platform; **multi-user collaboration in VR**; advanced AI assistants; **Sandbox mode for pharmaceutical design + compound reverse engineering**; **gamified student progress tracking**; customized learning paths; **haptic feedback**; learner-stats visualization.
- **Business Model (Figure 19):** Key partners = pharmacy schools, content developers, CHED. **Core resources = Unity platform, VR hardware, 3D pharma asset library.** Value props = safe/inexpensive lab sims, active learning, real-time assessment. Customer segments = students, instructors, institutions. Costs = VR content creation, licensing, teacher training, cloud infra. Revenue = institutional subscriptions, academic licensing, education grants.
- **Go-to-Market (Figure 18):** partner with tertiary institutions offering CHED-accredited pharmacy courses; integrate as supplement or alternative to existing lab courses.

### Appendix A — Storyboard
Contains **only image placeholders** (a long run of empty image cells in the txt export). No extractable text. **Design-relevant flag:** a visual storyboard exists as figures — the rebuild team should request the original doc/images, since scene flow/shot framing lives there but is invisible in this export.

### Appendix B — Rubrics (THE SCORING SPEC — highly build-relevant)
"Rubrics of every experiment as the basis of the assessment score." **5-level scale per criterion: Exemplary (5) / Proficient (4) / Good (3) / Needs Improvement (2) / Unacceptable (1),** summed to a **TOTAL SCORE.** Five criteria:
1. **Materials, Preparedness & Personal Safety** — equipment accurately placed; supplies neat/organized/on-hand; understanding of health & safety issues; **student wears complete PPE at all times** (degrades to incomplete/no PPE at lower levels).
2. **Procedure** — understanding of the procedure & amount of assistance needed; works in clean/organized manner; number of safety-measure reminders received (down to "disorderly from the start, no output presented").
3. **Time Management** — completeness of the assigned task **on time** (scaled: completes on time → completes most but late → does not complete).
4. **Sanitation & Clean-Up** — proper clean-up procedures; helping peers; **station left neat/clean** (down to items left uncleaned).
5. **Documentation** — organized/neat/legible; **post-lab questions answered correctly, in complete sentences with cited references** (scaled by number of questions answered wrong: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → >3).

**Implication for Unity scoring:** these five rubric axes are the natural mapping targets for the in-game Bayesian mastery score — i.e., the game should instrument PPE-worn state, correct equipment placement, procedure-step adherence, assistance/reminder counts, elapsed time vs. target, station cleanup at end, and a post-lab quiz. The 90% mastery gate presumably derives from a normalized aggregate of these.

## Appendix C (Part 1 of 2) — Pharmaceutical & Medicinal Organic Chemistry Laboratory Manual

This chunk is the **source-of-truth lab manual** that the VR experiments are modeled on. Institution: **World Citi Colleges, College of Pharmacy**. The manual has a title page (with student Name/Section fields), a Table of Contents, and full per-experiment write-ups (objectives, equipment, reagents, reagent preparation, step-by-step procedure, data sheets, post-lab questions, and a grading rubric). This chunk contains Experiments 1–4 in full (Exp 4 rubric is cut off at the end). Experiments 5–9 are in the next chunk (Appendix C part 2).

### Course structure / Table of Contents (9 experiments across 3 terms)
Organized by academic term — this maps directly to a **level/progression structure** for the VR game:
- **Prelim:** Exp 1 Stoichiometry (p.1); Exp 2 Chemical Reactions of Organic Compounds (p.8)
- **Midterm:** Exp 3 Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis (p.15); Exp 4 Benzoic Acid Synthesis (p.21); Exp 5 Acetanilide Synthesis (p.28); Exp 6 Acetone Synthesis (p.34)
- **Final:** Exp 7 Chloroform Synthesis (p.41); Exp 8 Benzamide Synthesis (p.47); Exp 9 Wine Making Activity (p.53)

A **recurring 5-level grading rubric** appears after every experiment (criteria: Materials/Preparedness/Personal Safety; Procedure; Time Management; Sanitation & Clean-Up; Documentation — scored Exemplary(5) → Proficient(4) → Good(3) → Needs Improvement(2) → Unacceptable(1), with a TOTAL SCORE). This rubric is a strong template for the VR **scoring/assessment system** (PPE compliance, procedure accuracy, cleanup, time management, correctly answered post-lab questions).

### EXPERIMENT 1 — STOICHIOMETRY (theory/calculation lab, no wet chemistry)
**Objectives:** balance chemical reactions; compute molecular weight of organic compounds; compute stoichiometry via dimensional analysis; compute percentage yield.

**Key concepts/definitions to surface as in-game tutorial/UI text:** limiting reagent, excess reactant, theoretical yield, actual yield, percentage yield.

**Formula:** Percentage Yield = (Actual Yield / Theoretical Yield) × 100.

**4-step procedure:** (1) balance the equation; (2) compute molecular weights of reactant & product; (3) compute theoretical yield; (4) compute percent yield.

**Worked example baked into the manual (reuse as tutorial/validation values):**
- Balance CH4 + O2 → CO2 + H2O ⇒ CH4 + 2O2 → CO2 + 2H2O (walkthrough uses an atom-count table for C/H/O across reactant vs product).
- MW of CH4 = 16 g/mol (C: 1×12=12; H: 4×1=4).
- Dimensional analysis general path: **Mass → Mole → Mass**.
- 15.0 g CH4 (excess O2) → theoretical **41.25 g CO2** (uses MW CO2 = 44 g/mol).
- If actual yield = 35 g CO2 ⇒ % yield = 35/41.25 × 100 = **84.85%**.

**Quiz/assessment content (Exp 1 worksheet) — directly usable as in-game problem bank:**
- Balance these 5: (1) NH3 + O2 → NO + H2O; (2) C3H5N3O9 + CO2 → N2 + O2 + H2O (nitroglycerin); (3) C3H7OH + O2 → CO2 + H2O; (4) C6H12O6 + O2 → CO2 + H2O (glucose); (5) C2H6 + O2 → H2O + CO2.
- Solve (show solution, encircle final answer):
  1. 4.95 g Ethylene (C2H4) combusted with 3.25 g O2 → (a) limiting reagent? (b) grams CO2 formed?
  2. 10 g Salicylic acid + 25 ml acetic anhydride → (a) grams Aspirin? (b) if actual = 5.20 g, % yield?
  3. 25 g Aspartame (C14H18N2O5) + water → aspartic acid (C4H7NO4) + methanol (CH3OH) + phenylalanine (C9H11NO2) → (a) grams phenylalanine? (b) if actual = 12 g, % yield?
  4. 15 g Salicylic acid + 25 ml Methanol → Methyl Salicylate → (a) grams produced? (b) if actual = 14 g, % yield?

Worksheet header fields to model in UI: Name / Date / Group Number / Score.

---

### EXPERIMENT 2 — CHEMICAL REACTIONS OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS (qualitative functional-group tests)
**Objectives:** write chemical reactions for alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters; differentiate tests for each class.

**Equipment/Apparatus (glassware set for VR asset list):** Aspirator, Beaker, Erlenmeyer flask, Pipette, Porcelain spatula, Stirring rod, Test tube, Test tube brush, Test tube holder.

**Reagents:** 0.1% Potassium permanganate, 10% Ferric chloride, 10% Sodium bicarbonate, 6N Sodium hydroxide, 6N Sulfuric acid, Concentrated sulfuric acid, Concentrated hydrochloric acid, Diluted acetic acid, Acetaldehyde, Acetone, Aspirin, Benedict's reagent, Benzyl alcohol, Ethyl alcohol, Glycerol, Methanol, n-butyl alcohol, Phenol, Salicylic acid, Sec-butyl alcohol, Ter(t)-butyl alcohol, Tollen's reagent.

**Reagent preparation (with embedded author color/index annotations — see NOTE below):**
- 0.1% KMnO4 → dissolve 0.1 g in 100 ml purified water. (annotated "violet — 0")
- 10% FeCl3 → 10 g in 100 ml water. (annotated "brown — 1")
- 10% NaHCO3 → 10 g in 100 ml water. (annotated "white — 2")
- 6N NaOH → 24 g in 100 ml water in a volumetric flask. (annotated "3")
- 6N H2SO4 → mix 33.3 ml conc. H2SO4 into 66.7 ml water in a volumetric flask. (annotated "4")

**Procedure (4 test groups):**
1. **Alcohols**
   - *Test for Enol Structure* (tag "10%-5"): place 1 ml each of ethanol, n-butyl alcohol, benzyl alcohol, phenol, glycerol in separate test tubes; dilute with 10 ml distilled water; add 5 drops 10% FeCl3; note color produced in each. (Expected: phenol/enols give characteristic FeCl3 color.)
   - *Rate of Oxidation* (tag "10%-6", note "i follow youtube instruction"): 2 ml 0.1% KMnO4 + 2 drops 6N NaOH in four test tubes; add 5 drops each of (a) n-butyl, (b) sec-butyl, (c) tert-butyl alcohol into 3 tubes, 4th = negative control; shake, observe color change; repeat with 2 ml 0.1% KMnO4 + 2 drops 6N H2SO4; **note time of decolorization** for each tube.
2. **Aldehydes and Ketones**
   - *Oxidation* (tag "10%-7"): 2 drops acetone + 2 drops acetaldehyde in separate tubes; add 2 ml Tollen's reagent to each; heat 5 min in water bath; observe color change (silver mirror for aldehyde).
3. **Carboxylic Acid** (tag "10%-8")
   - *Ester Formation*: 10 drops ethanol + 4 drops diluted acetic acid + 1 drop conc. H2SO4 in a test tube; warm in water bath; note odor; repeat using methyl alcohol + 0.1 g salicylic acid + conc. H2SO4; note odor after pouring into a beaker containing 1 ml distilled water. (Expected: fruity/wintergreen ester odors.)
4. **Esters**
   - *Hydrolysis* (tag "10%-9", steps tagged 14-0…14-3): 0.5 g acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) in a test tube; add 10 ml distilled water + 1 drop conc. HCl; boil gently 3–5 min in water bath; filter off undissolved crystals; add 2 drops 10% FeCl3 to filtrate; repeat with **unhydrolyzed** aspirin and compare (hydrolyzed gives positive FeCl3 test for liberated salicylic acid).

**Data Sheet structure (per organic-compound class):** columns = Organic Compound | Tests | Chemical Reactions Involved | Results (Changes in color). Rows enumerate each sample (Ethyl alcohol, n-butyl, glycerol, benzyl, phenol; the four oxidation tubes incl. negative control; acetone/acetaldehyde with Tollen's AND Benedict's reagent; ester-formation pairs; hydrolyzed vs unhydrolyzed aspirin).

**Post-lab questions:** (1) What are organic compounds? (2) What are functional groups? (3) Tabulate classes (Alcohol, Aldehyde, Carboxylic acid, Ester, Ketone) with functional group + examples. All require cited references.

### EXPERIMENT 3 — ETHYL ALCOHOL SYNTHESIS (fermentation + distillation)
**Objectives:** synthesize ethyl alcohol; confirm identity via chemical tests.

**Equipment (distillation rig — key VR asset set):** Aspirator, S-clamp, Beaker, Stirring rod, Condenser, Test tubes, Cork, Test tube brush, Distilling flask, Test tube holder, Erlenmeyer flask, Thermometer, Iron stand, Iron clamp, Pipette, Watch glass, Porcelain spatula, Water bath, Rubber tubing.

**Reagents:** 6% Sodium hypochlorite, 10% Potassium iodide, 10% Sodium hydroxide, Ammonium phosphate, Brown sugar, Concentrated sulfuric acid, Diluted acetic acid, Dry yeast, Ethanol, Saturated calcium hydroxide (lime water).

**Reagent prep:** 10% KI → 10 g in 100 ml water; 10% NaOH → 10 g in 100 ml water in volumetric flask.

**Procedure — Synthesis (weighted 40%):**
1. Prepare 100 ml of 12% brown sugar solution in a flask.
2. Weigh 4 g active dry yeast into a beaker.
3. Add a little sugar solution to make a thin paste.
4. Transfer paste to flask (include rinsings).
5. Add a pinch of ammonium phosphate.
6. Shake; attach a bent tube fitted to a stopper to bubble evolved CO2 into lime water.
7. Lead the bent tube into a test tube containing 10–15 ml (lime water).
8. Place a cotton swab on top of the test tube.
9. Let stand **one full week** at room temperature.
10. Do not shake; decant liquid into a 125 ml distilling flask.
11. Adjust pH slightly alkaline (litmus) using 10% NaOH.
12. Distill until 1/2 to 1/3 of original volume remains.
13. Test flammability: 2–3 drops distillate on watch glass + lighted matchstick.
14. Fractionally distill, collecting the **70–80 °C** fraction into a 10 ml graduated cylinder.
15. Record distillate volume.

**Procedure — Chemical Tests (weighted 40%):**
16. **Combustion Test:** 1 ml distillate on watch glass + lighted matchstick — **blue flame = complete combustion (CO2 + H2O), yellow flame = incomplete.**
17. **Iodoform Test:** 15 ml 10% KI + 1 ml distillate in a test tube; add 8 ml 6% NaOCl; warm 5 min in water bath at **60 °C**; shake occasionally; let stand (positive = yellow iodoform precipitate).
18. **Ester Formation:** 20 drops ethanol + 10 drops diluted acetic acid + 5 drops conc. H2SO4; warm in water bath; note odor.

**Data Sheet:** balanced chemical reaction; Experimental Data table (Reactants/Products with Molecular Weight, Density, Amount Used/Produced); Calculations (Actual Yield, Theoretical Yield, Percentage Yield, Computations); Physical Tests (Appearance, Color, Specific Gravity, Boiling Point); Chemical Tests results (Combustion flame color + reaction; Iodoform Step 1/Step 2/Result; Ester Formation reaction + result).

**Post-lab questions:** (1) How do (a) concentration, (b) structure, (c) molecular weight affect antiseptic activity of alcohols? (2) Mechanism: ethanol abuse → liver cirrhosis; methanol → blindness. (3) Mechanism by which Disulfiram treats alcoholism. All need cited references.

**Objectives:** synthesize benzoic acid; confirm identity via chemical tests.

**Equipment:** identical distillation-rig list as Exp 3.

**Reagents:** 0.1% Potassium permanganate, Benzoic acid, 6N Hydrochloric acid, 6N Sodium hydroxide, 10% Sodium hydroxide, Concentrated sulfuric acid, Methanol, Propyl alcohol, Benzaldehyde.

(2) How do preservatives prevent deterioration; at what pH are benzoic acid and its salts effective as preservatives in foods/pharma? (3) Metabolic changes of benzoic acid. Rubric follows (text cut off mid-rubric — continues in next chunk).

## EXPERIMENT 5 — ACETANILIDE SYNTHESIS
**Objectives:** synthesize acetanilide; determine identity via chemical tests.
**Reagents:** 0.1N Hydrochloric acid, Bromine water, Acetyl chloride, Concentrated hydrochloric acid, Aniline, Glacial acetic acid.
**Preparation of Reagents — 0.1N HCl (20%):** Take 2.1 ml concentrated HCl, slowly dilute to 250 ml purified water in a volumetric flask.
**Procedure 1 — Synthesis (40%):**
1. In the **fume hood**, place 2 ml aniline in a Florence flask (flat bottom).
2. Add 2 ml glacial acetic acid.
3. Carefully add 2 ml acetyl chloride.
4. Heat flask gently ~5 min using a water bath.
5. Cool 5 min.
6. Add 20 ml ice-cold water.
7. Filter off crystals.
8. Wash thrice with water.
9. Dry crystals and weigh.
10. Calculate percentage yield.
**Procedure 2 — Chemical Tests (40%):**
1. **Hydrolysis of acetanilide:** 1 g acetanilide + 3 ml conc HCl in test tube; boil carefully 1 min in water bath; cool, dilute with water; note appearance of crystals.
2. **Bromination:** 1 g acetanilide + 5 ml water + 0.1 ml conc HCl; add bromine water until distinctly yellow. Repeat using aniline. Compare volume of bromine needed to reach yellow.
**Post-lab Qs:** (1) Chemical name of acetanilide + reaction to litmus? (2) How does structure of amine differ from amide? (3) Undesirable/toxic effects of acetanilide; explain biotransformation of acetanilide.

## EXPERIMENT 6 — ACETONE SYNTHESIS
**Reagents:** 5% Sodium hypochlorite, Saturated sodium bisulfite, 10% Potassium hydroxide solution, Schiff's reagent, Calcium acetate, Sodium acetate, Ethyl alcohol, Tollen's reagent.
**Preparation of Reagents — 10% KOH (20%):** Dissolve 10 g KOH in 100 ml purified water.
**Procedure 1 — Synthesis (40%):** (dry distillation of acetate salts)
1. Weigh 7 g Calcium acetate + 7 g anhydrous Sodium acetate; mix both powders, put in a test tube.
2. Set up tube in **horizontal** position, connect with condenser.
3. Tap tube gently so a channel forms along its whole length.
4. Heat until the acetates glow, turning the tube periodically; all salts heated at higher temperature.
5. Distill and collect distillate **at 56°C**.
6. Weigh distillate, calculate % yield.
**Procedure 2 — Chemical Tests (40%):**
1. **Tollen's Test:** few drops acetone in 5 ml water + Tollen's reagent (ammoniacal silver nitrate); note color.
2. **Schiff's Reagent:** few drops acetone + 5 ml Schiff's reagent; examine after standing.
3. **Sodium hypochlorite (iodoform-type):** 10 ml 10% potassium iodide in test tube + 2 drops sample; while shaking add 10 ml 5–7% sodium hypochlorite; heat in water bath; add iodide drop by drop until permanent brownish color; observe appearance and odor.
4. **Bisulfite addition products:** 0.5 ml ethyl alcohol + 2 ml saturated sodium bisulfite; filter off precipitate; add 0.5 ml acetone; shake vigorously 1 min; place in freezing mixture; observe color/appearance change.
**Post-lab Qs:** (1) Functional group common to aldehydes and ketones? (2) Name 5 drug preparations classified as ketone + pharmacologic uses. (3) What is ketonuria? Pathophysiology.

## EXPERIMENT 7 — CHLOROFORM SYNTHESIS
**Reagents:** Acetone, Concentrated sulfuric acid, Alcoholic silver nitrate, Potassium dichromate, Bleaching powder.
**Procedure 1 — Synthesis (50%):** (haloform reaction; note this synthesis is weighted 50/50, no separate reagent-prep weight)
1. Place 150 g fresh bleaching powder + 400 ml water in a 1-Liter flask.
2. Shake vigorously until bleaching powder dissolves.
3. Connect flask with a reflux condenser.
4. Mix 12 g acetone + 50 ml water in a separatory funnel.
5. Add acetone-water mixture drop by drop through the condenser.
6. Arrange flask for distillation setup.
7. Once chloroform produced, wash via decantation with water; repeat decantation twice.
8. Place chloroform in a small distilling flask containing anhydrous calcium chloride.
9. Continue distillation until liquid is clear and no water droplets visible.
10. Collect distillate, weigh.
11. Calculate % yield.
**Procedure 2 — Chemical Tests (50%):**
1. **Inflammability:** try to ignite chloroform; light a match near the vapors; observe. (Design note: expected result is non-flammable — a safety/teaching beat.)
2. **Oxidation:** heat few drops chloroform + small portion potassium bichromate crystals + 2 ml conc sulfuric acid; note odor produced.
3. **Reaction with alcoholic silver nitrate:** 1–2 drops chloroform + 1 ml alcoholic silver nitrate; note reaction; if no precipitate after 5 min, heat in water bath; note time precipitate forms. (Time-to-precipitate is a measurable outcome — good for a timer mechanic.)
**Post-lab Qs:** (1) Uses of chloroform in medicine and industry? (2) Why protect chloroform from sunlight? Give specific product of the reaction. (3) How does chloroform exposure lead to liver damage? Discuss biotransformation of chloroform.

## EXPERIMENT 8 — BENZAMIDE SYNTHESIS
**Reagents:** 10% Sodium hydroxide, Concentrated ammonia, 10% Sodium nitrate solution, Diluted hydrochloric acid, Benzoyl chloride. (Note internal inconsistency: reagent list says "10% Sodium nitrate solution" but Test 3 procedure uses "10% Sodium nitrite solution" — flag for chemistry accuracy.)
**Procedure 1 — Synthesis (50%):**
1. Place 5 ml concentrated ammonia solution in a 50-ml flask.
2. Cool in an ice bath.
3. Add 1 ml benzoyl chloride drop by drop; shake flask frequently while dropping.
4. Allow to stand 10–15 minutes.
5. Filter off the precipitate.
6. Wash using cold water.
7. Collect crystals, dry using an oven.
8. Calculate % yield.
**Procedure 2 — Chemical Tests (50%):**
1. **Hydrolysis using an alkali:** 2 ml 10% NaOH + 0.5 g benzamide in test tube; heat to boiling in water bath; test pH of vapor with litmus paper.
2. **Acid hydrolysis:** 2 ml diluted HCl + 0.5 g benzamide; test pH of vapor with litmus paper.
3. **Action of nitrous acid:** 2–3 ml diluted HCl + 0.5 g benzamide; then add 3 ml 10% sodium nitrite solution; note any changes.
**Post-lab Qs:** (1) Write reactions illustrating methods of preparing amides: (a) Ammonium acetate + heat, (b) Ethyl acetate + ammonium hydroxide, (c) Hydrolysis of propylnitrile. (2) Name 5 drug products classified as amides + pharmacologic uses. (3) Illustrate biotransformation pathways of amide drugs.

## EXPERIMENT 9 — WINE MAKING ACTIVITY (final / capstone experiment — different format, no standard rubric block)
This is a **group project + event**, not a bench synthesis — likely a distinct game mode / finale scene rather than a lab level.
**Guidelines:**
1. Final experiment for the course.
2. Objective: learn basic methodology of preparing/synthesizing alcohol via **fermentation** using basic household kitchen ingredients.
3. Per-group basis (professor-determined).
4. Groups of 3–5 members, tasks assigned by leader.
5. Task distribution examples: (a) performs fermentation w/ video documentation, (b) edits documented video, (c) creates label + documentation, (d) other essential tasks.
6. Ferment a wine: **minimum 250 mL, maximum 500 mL**, fruit of choice (**grapes excluded**).
7. Bottles suitable to specified amount.
8. Documentation: **15–20 minute video** showing wine preparation methods.
9. **Wine tasting event** conducted by students as part of the **final examination**.
10. **Wine Tasting Grading Rubric:** Workmanship (Prep of Ingredients, Methodology) 15% | General Appearance (Wine bottle) 20% | Presentation (Table presentation, Costume of presenters) 20% | Photo and Video Documentation 20% | Flavor, Smell and Taste 25%.

## References (chunk ends here, ~byte 165,390)
Academic reference list. Design-relevant VR/education & pedagogy sources the previous team built PharmaSynth on:
- **Abuhammad et al. (2021), "MedChemVR": A virtual reality game to enhance medicinal chemistry education** (MTI 5(3):10) — closest prior-art VR game; likely a primary design reference.
- Chang et al. (2025) VR simulation for undergraduate nursing (JMIR Med Educ).

Plus many medicinal-chemistry domain references (PROTACs, retrosynthesis AI, drug combinations, biocatalysis, pharmacogenomics) — background for narrative/educational flavor, not mechanics.

## Design takeaways for the Unity rebuild
- **Levels 5–8 share one reusable lab environment** (identical apparatus list) — build one modular lab-bench scene + swap reagent racks and procedure scripts per experiment.
- **Each experiment = a scripted state machine** with two graded phases: Synthesis and Chemical Tests, each phase carrying an explicit % weight (40/40, 50/50) that maps 1:1 to in-game scoring. Some experiments add a Reagent-Preparation phase (20%).
- **Data Sheet UI** is a consistent form the player fills: reactant/product tables (MW, density, amount), yield calculations (actual/theoretical/percentage), physical tests (appearance, color, specific gravity, boiling point), and per-test reaction/result fields. Reuse one templated UI panel.
- **Scoring/rubric system** is uniform: 5 criteria (Safety+PPE, Procedure, Time Management, Sanitation/Clean-Up, Documentation) on a 1–5 scale; Documentation score is tied to # of correct post-lab quiz answers. This is a ready-made scoring model.
- **Safety mechanics** are explicit and gradable: mandatory PPE at all times, fume-hood use (Exp 5 aniline/acetyl chloride), ice baths, "carefully"/"gently" steps, flammability test (Exp 7). These support hazard/error-handling systems and penalty triggers.
- **Measurable outcomes** for feedback/verification: distillate collected at 56°C (acetone), color change to yellow (bromination), permanent brownish color (iodoform), time-to-precipitate (chloroform + AgNO3), litmus pH of vapor (benzamide), crystal appearance (hydrolysis).
- **Experiment 9 (wine/fermentation)** is a group event/finale with its own rubric — treat as a distinct capstone mode, possibly narrative/celebratory rather than a bench-sim level.
- **Post-lab question banks** (3 per experiment) are ready content for in-game quizzes/assessment gates.
- **Data note:** Appendix D (syllabus) and Appendix E (data-gathering proof) are images only — if the team needs their content, they must be OCR'd from the original doc/images, not the txt export.

Every bench experiment (5-8) follows a fixed template: Objectives, identical Equipment list, Reagents, optional Reagent-Prep, Procedure split into graded Synthesis (40-50%) + Chemical Tests (40-50%) phases, a Data Sheet form, 3 post-lab questions, and an identical 5-criteria grading rubric.

Universal Laboratory Report Grading Rubric: 5 criteria (Materials/Preparedness/Personal Safety incl. mandatory PPE; Procedure; Time Management; Sanitation & Clean-Up; Documentation) scored Exemplary(5) to Unacceptable(1); Documentation score is driven by number of post-lab questions answered correctly.

Exp 5 Acetanilide: fume-hood synthesis from 2ml aniline + 2ml glacial acetic acid + 2ml acetyl chloride, heat 5 min water bath, cool, add 20ml ice-cold water, filter/wash/dry/weigh; tests = hydrolysis (conc HCl) and bromination vs aniline.

Exp 6 Acetone: dry distillation of 7g calcium acetate + 7g sodium acetate in a horizontal test tube, collect distillate at 56C; tests = Tollen's, Schiff's, sodium hypochlorite/iodoform, bisulfite addition.

Exp 7 Chloroform: haloform reaction of 150g bleaching powder + 400ml water with 12g acetone + 50ml water added dropwise, reflux then distill over anhydrous CaCl2; tests = inflammability (match near vapor), oxidation (K dichromate + conc H2SO4), alcoholic AgNO3 with time-to-precipitate.

Exp 8 Benzamide: 5ml conc ammonia in ice bath + 1ml benzoyl chloride dropwise, stand 10-15 min, filter/wash/oven-dry; tests = alkali hydrolysis, acid hydrolysis, action of nitrous acid; reagent list says 'sodium nitrate' but procedure uses 'sodium nitrite' (inconsistency to flag).

Exp 9 Wine Making is the capstone/finale: group (3-5) fermentation project, 250-500 mL non-grape fruit wine, 15-20 min documentation video, ending in a graded wine-tasting event (Workmanship 15%, Appearance 20%, Presentation 20%, Documentation 20%, Flavor/Smell/Taste 25%).

Data Sheet UI schema (reusable): reactant table (MW/density/amount used), product table (MW/density/amount produced), calculations (actual/theoretical/percentage yield + computations), physical tests (appearance/color/specific gravity/boiling point), per-test reaction + result fields, Name/Score/Date/Group No header.

Measurable success signals for feedback: 56C distillate cutoff, yellow endpoint in bromination, permanent brownish color in iodoform, litmus pH of evolved vapor, and time-to-precipitate with alcoholic AgNO3.

Each page = a full-screen 2D mockup of a VR moment: a photorealistic/3D rendered background (first-person VR view with the player's robotic hands visible), plus overlaid vector UI text (dialogue, menu labels, HUD) and small element images (Pharmee mascot, Settings gear, tablet, reagent labels). ### Experiment 1 tablet content (verbatim, from P33)
**Title: "Methane Synthesis"**
- **Materials Needed:** Sodium acetate (CH3COONa); Soda lime (NaOH + CaO mixture); Distilled water (for testing); Limewater (Ca(OH)2 solution, for gas identification).
- **Apparatuses:** Test tube (hard glass); Bunsen burner; Delivery tube; Water trough; Beaker or test tube (for gas collection); Clamp and stand; Rubber cork with hole.
- **Procedures (5 steps, each ~=20% progress):**
  1. *Preparation of Mixture:* Mix 2 parts soda lime with 1 part sodium acetate in a clean, dry test tube.
  2. *Setup:* Fit the test tube with a delivery tube and rubber stopper. Connect the delivery tube to a water-filled trough with an inverted test tube or gas jar ready for gas collection.
  3. *Heating:* Gently heat the test tube using a Bunsen burner, starting from the top to avoid sudden pressure build-up.
  4. *Gas Collection:* Collect the evolved gas (methane) over water. Discard the first few bubbles (air displacement), then collect the methane in the inverted gas jar.
  5. *Testing the Gas:* Bring a burning splint near the mouth of the test tube or collected jar. A blue flame with slight pop indicates the presence of methane.
- **Chemical Reaction:** CH3COONa + NaOH --(heat)--> CH4 + Na2CO3
- Tablet layout: two columns (Materials/Apparatuses left, Procedures right, each with a checkbox), decorative blue tech corner brackets, reaction equation footer with a small hazard icon.

### Characters / NPCs
- **Pharmee** — the guide/mascot: a cute floating white+light-blue robot, rounded rectangular head as a black screen "face" showing glowing cyan eyes (curved happy eyes when greeting; round eyes when presenting), small side ears/antennae, detached floating hands, a glowing core on the torso. Has at least 2 poses: (a) greeting/arms-open, (b) assessment holding checklist + pen. Delivers all tutorial narration.
- **Dr. Jimenez** — human examiner: brown hair, glasses, white lab coat, blue shirt + tie. Silent observer who grades performance and explicitly gives NO hints. Appears standing across the bench.

### UI / HUD
- **Main menu:** Tutorial, Settings, Quit, Laboratory (over the door/title art).
- **In-lab HUD (screen-space overlay in mockups):** top-left **Settings** button with a 3D grey **gear icon**; a **Progress** label + horizontal **progress bar** with **% readout** (0/20/40/60/80/100); a **timer "00:00:00"** (HH:MM:SS count-up).
- **Tablet UI:** diegetic handheld tablet showing the per-experiment instruction sheet (materials, apparatus, numbered procedures w/ checkboxes, reaction equation).
- **Settings contents:** audio levels, display preferences, text size, other accessibility options.

### Environment / props (art references)
- Rooms: (1) PPE/locker ante-room (grey gym lockers, vision-panel swing door); (2) main lab with blue base cabinets, cream walls, dark skirting, drop-ceiling panel lights, wall cabinets, sink, island benches, framed picture, grey vision-panel door; (3) equipment cabinet (labeled open shelving); (4) apparatus bench (glassware + instruments + coat stand).
- Player embodiment: first-person **robotic/prosthetic white+navy articulated VR hands with glowing purple/blue wrist rings** (no full avatar body — PPE is not shown on a third-person body).
- Reagent bottles modeled with labels: Bromine water, Ethanol, Limewater (plus dropper bottles). Glassware: beakers, Erlenmeyer flasks, graduated cylinders, watch glasses, funnels, test tubes/racks, separating funnel, clamp stand.

### Mechanics implied for the rebuild
- Flow: Menu -> Tutorial(training) -> PPE (locker) -> guided tour (waypoint markers) -> grab tablet -> gather equipment (labeled cabinet) + ingredients (ingredients cabinet) + apparatus (table) -> perform steps -> progress bar advances per completed step -> timer runs throughout.
- **Assessment + hard gating:** each procedure step is validated; a mistake forces a redo and blocks progression until done correctly. Grading is on procedural correctness; Dr. Jimenez observes but never hints.
- Progress model: 5 steps mapped to 20% each = 100% for Experiment 1. Timer is count-up (challenge/time-management, but "accuracy over speed").

**Experiment 1 tablet card — "Chemical Compounding" (verbatim, pages 56/64/66/68/70):**
- *Materials Needed:* Ethanol (C₂H₅OH); Bromine water (Br₂ in water); Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) solution; Dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄); Hydrochloric acid (HCl); Sodium metal (Na); Limewater (Ca(OH)₂ solution); Distilled water.
- *Apparatuses:* Test tubes and test tube rack; Beaker; Watch glass; Dropper or pipette; Bunsen burner; Tongs; Measuring cylinder; Thermometer; Glass rod.
- *Procedures (each with a checkbox):*
  1. **Combustion of Ethanol** — Pour a small amount of ethanol into a test tube and transfer it to a watch glass. Ignite the ethanol using a Bunsen burner and observe the clean blue flame, indicating complete combustion.
  2. **Reaction with Sodium** — Add a small piece of sodium metal to a fresh test tube containing ethanol. Observe the rapid effervescence, which indicates the release of hydrogen gas.
  3. **Bromine Water Test** — In a new test tube, add ethanol or an alkene (if provided), followed by a few drops of bromine water. Shake gently and observe if the brown color disappears. Decolorization indicates the presence of an unsaturated compound.
  4. **Oxidation Test with KMnO₄** — Add a few drops of potassium permanganate and dilute sulfuric acid to a test tube containing ethanol. Heat gently and observe if the purple color fades to colorless, confirming oxidation to acetic acid.
  (Card styling: bold black title, blue tech-corner brackets, checkboxes down the left of each material and each procedure.)

---

### Pages 73–82 — PRELIM Experiment 2 ("Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis")

- **p73 — Experiment 2 intro/title card.** Overlay: **"Chemical Reaction of Organic Compounds" / "Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis" / "Prelim Term"** plus a large **"Ethyl Alcohol"** watermark (shown twice). HUD `Progress 0%`, timer `00:00:00`. Scene = lab interior.

**Experiment 2 tablet card — "Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis" (verbatim, pages 76/78/80/82):**
- *Materials Needed:* Glucose or sucrose (sugar source); Dry baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae); Distilled water; Limewater (for gas test); Dilute sulfuric acid (optional, to prevent bacterial growth).
- *Apparatuses:* Conical flask; Delivery tube and stopper; Water bath; Beaker or test tube; Fermentation lock (or substitute setup using tubing and limewater); Measuring cylinder; Thermometer.
- *Procedures (checkboxes):*
  1. **Preparation of Fermentation Mixture** — Dissolve ~10–20 g of glucose or sucrose in 100 mL of warm distilled water (~30–35 °C) in a conical flask.
  2. **Addition of Yeast** — Add a small amount of dry yeast (~1 g) to the sugar solution. Swirl gently to mix.
  3. **Anaerobic Setup** — Fit the flask with a stopper and delivery tube, leading into a test tube of limewater to trap and detect CO₂ production. This ensures anaerobic (oxygen-free) conditions.
  4. **Fermentation** — Keep the setup in a warm area (30–35 °C) for 2–3 days. Observe bubbling, indicating fermentation and CO₂ release.
  5. **Observation of Completion** — Once bubbling slows down or stops, fermentation is complete. The solution now contains ethanol and water.
- *Reaction (printed on card):* **C₆H₁₂O₆ —(yeast)→ 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂** (Glucose → Ethanol + Carbon Dioxide).

## PharmaSynth Storyboard — Pages 83–123 Digest

This range covers the **end of the Prelim period** (the final Prelim experiment, *Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis*, reaching 100% + its results screens), the **Period-Selection hub**, the **Midterm period menu**, and the **first two Midterm experiments in full** — *Benzoic Acid Synthesis* (pp.88–107) and *Acetanilide Synthesis* (pp.108–123). All frames are first-person VR renders (Meta Quest) with two white/navy **robot hands** (glowing purple wrist ring) reaching toward a lab bench. Extracted page-render images live in `.../scratchpad/storyboard-3/`.

### Recurring UI / HUD (visible on nearly every experiment page)
- **Top-left HUD:** a **Settings** label backed by a 3D **gear/cog icon** (asset `p88_4`).
- **Progress tracker:** a **"Progress"** label with a **percentage** (0%–100%) and a horizontal progress bar; advances as experiment steps are completed.
- **Timer:** a running clock shown as **`00:00:00`** (HH:MM:SS), top area.
- **Tablet device asset** (`p88_3`): a grey-bezel blank tablet — the in-world surface that displays the **procedure card / checklist** (see below). No separate wristwatch UI was seen in this range; the wrist has only a glowing ring on the hand model.

### Procedure Card UI (the most important reusable UI element)
Each experiment shows a full-panel **procedure card** (blue tech-framed white panel, `_4` overlay images) with **three checkbox checklists** + a balanced equation. Structure = **Materials Needed** (checkboxes), **Apparatuses** (checkboxes), **Procedures** (numbered checkboxes), and a **Balanced Chemical Reaction** footer. These are the checklist/progress-tracker mechanic; each checked item corresponds to progress advancement.

**Benzoic Acid Synthesis card (`p90_4`):**
- Materials: Toluene (C₆H₅CH₃); Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄); Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) or distilled water; Hydrochloric acid (HCl), dilute; Ice; Ethanol (optional, for purification).
- Apparatuses: Round-bottom flask; Reflux condenser; Beaker; Funnel and filter paper; Glass rod; Bunsen burner or hot plate; Water bath; Thermometer.
- Procedures: 1) Oxidation Reaction Setup — mix 5 mL toluene with 100 mL aqueous KMnO₄ (saturated or 0.02 mol) in round-bottom flask, add a little sodium carbonate for basicity. 2) Reflux Heating — attach reflux condenser, gently heat in water bath 1–2 h. 3) Observation — purple KMnO₄ fades, brown MnO₂ precipitate forms. 4) Filtration of MnO₂ — cool and filter off precipitate. 5) Acidification — add dilute HCl to filtrate while stirring until pH < 7; benzoic acid precipitates as white solid. 6) Cooling & Crystallization — ice bath. 7) Filtration & Washing — filter crystals, wash with cold water.
- Equation: C₆H₅CH₃ + 2[O] —(KMnO₄)→ C₆H₅COOH + H₂O (Toluene → Benzoic Acid).

**Acetanilide Synthesis card (`p111_4`):**
- Materials: Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂); Acetic anhydride (C₄H₆O₃); Glacial acetic acid (optional, solvent); Distilled water; Ice.
- Apparatuses: Conical flask; Beaker; Stirring rod; Dropper or pipette; Ice bath; Funnel and filter paper; Measuring cylinder; Glass rod.
- Procedures: 1) Mixing Reactants — 5 mL aniline + 5 mL acetic anhydride in clean conical flask, optionally 2–3 mL glacial acetic acid. 2) Reaction Initiation — stir gently with glass rod; acetylation starts immediately. 3) Cooling & Crystallization — pour slowly into beaker with crushed ice + 50–100 mL cold water, stir; white crystals precipitate. 4) Filtration — filter with paper + funnel, wash residue with cold water. 5) Drying — dry crystals on watch glass at room temp or gentle heat.
- Equation: C₆H₅NH₂ + (CH₃CO)₂O → C₆H₅NHCOCH₃ + CH₃COOH (Aniline + Acetic Anhydride → Acetanilide + Acetic Acid).

**Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis card (`p84_4`, the Prelim final experiment):**
- Materials: Glucose or sucrose; Dry baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae); Distilled water; Limewater (gas test); Dilute sulfuric acid (optional, anti-bacterial).
- Apparatuses: Conical flask; Delivery tube and stopper; Water bath; Beaker or test tube; Fermentation lock (or tubing+limewater substitute); Measuring cylinder; Thermometer.
- Procedures: 1) Preparation — dissolve 10–20 g sugar in 100 mL warm distilled water (~30–35 °C). 2) Addition of Yeast — add ~1 g dry yeast, swirl. 3) Anaerobic Setup — stopper + delivery tube into limewater test tube to trap/detect CO₂. 4) Fermentation — keep warm (30–35 °C) 2–3 days, observe bubbling. 5) Observation of Completion — bubbling slows/stops; solution now contains ethanol + water.
- Equation: C₆H₁₂O₆ —(yeast)→ 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (Glucose → Ethanol + Carbon Dioxide).

### NPC / Mascot
- **"Pharmee"** (named on p87): a friendly floating **white/blue robot assistant** (`p86_6`, `p87_4`) — rounded body, screen face with glowing cyan **happy arc eyes (^ ^)**, floating ear-fins and hands, glowing cyan chest button. Appears as the guide/host on the Period-Selection screen delivering dialogue.
- **Pharmee dialogue (p87 text):** *"You are now ready to take the midterm period...."*

### Page-by-page

**pp.83–86 — Prelim period FINAL experiment (Ethyl Alcohol) completion + results**
- p83–p84: HUD Progress **100%**, timer 00:00:00. Scene (`p84_0`) = reagent bench with bottles labeled **"YEAST" (Yordit Yeast)**, **"DISTILLLD VELET"** (typo for Distilled …), **"DISTILLED WATER"**, an amber-liquid flask in a **water bath with thermometer + condenser arm** (fermentation/distillation apparatus). `p84_4` = Ethyl Alcohol procedure card.
- p85: **"Mission Complete"** banner over the lab scene (`p85_0` = robot hands at bench, amber-liquid Erlenmeyer flask on a black stand with a curved condenser tube — distillation of the fermented product).
- p86: **Grade / results screen.** On-screen text: **"Mission Complete"**, **"Grade 100%"**, **"Number of Mistakes 0"**, **"Time 15:20"**, buttons **"Continue"** and **"Retry"**. (Values are large stylized numerals; `p86_5` is a decorative gold "7", `p123_1` shows the big "100%" grade numeral rendered blurred as background.)

**p87 — Period Selection hub**
- Scene (`p87_0`): a room with **three grey doors**, each with a window showing a lab inside → the three periods **Prelim / Midterm / Final** (labels in page text). Pharmee mascot present with dialogue "You are now ready to take the midterm period...". Player presumably walks to / selects a door. Settings HUD present.

**p88 — Midterm period MENU**
- Scene (`p88_0`): a clean, fully-stocked chemistry lab (central bench: beakers, Erlenmeyer flask, blue **digital scale/hotplate**, white basin; back counters: distillation stand, sink, glass-front cabinets, stools, exit door).
- Menu lists **four Midterm experiments**, each with name + "…Synthesis" subtitle: **Benzoic Acid**, **Acetanilide**, **Acetone**, **Chloroform** (Acetone & Chloroform fall on later pages beyond p123). Progress **0%**. Assets on this page: player hand model (`p88_1/2`), tablet (`p88_3`), Settings gear (`p88_4`).

**pp.89–107 — Midterm Experiment 1: Benzoic Acid**
- p89: **Cutscene** — text overlay `[CUTSCENE: EXPLAINING THE TASK AND THE OBJECTIVE OUTCOME]`, title **"Synthesis of Benzoic Acid"**, over a **blurred** version of the midterm lab (`p89_0`). Cutscene = task/objective briefing before hands-on steps.

- p100–p101 (70%): filtrate flask + a bottle labeled **"HYDROCHLORIC ACID"**, small beaker of dark filtrate (`p100_0`). = Acidification to precipitate benzoic acid.
- p102–p103 (80%): beaker of **white crystals** in a water bath with condenser (`p102_0`). = Cooling/crystallization.

key_facts: Pages 83-123 span the end of the Prelim period (Ethyl Alcohol Synthesis, its final experiment) and the first two Midterm experiments in full: Benzoic Acid (pp.88-107) and Acetanilide (pp.108-123).

The Midterm period has FOUR experiments listed in its menu (p88/p108): Benzoic Acid, Acetanilide, Acetone, Chloroform (each shown as name + '...Synthesis'). Acetone and Chloroform pages are beyond p123.

Benzoic Acid = KMnO4 oxidation of toluene: reflux (purple->brown MnO2), filter MnO2, acidify filtrate with dilute HCl, crystallize on ice, filter/wash. Equation C6H5CH3 + 2[O] -> C6H5COOH + H2O.

Acetanilide = acetylation of aniline with acetic anhydride (optional glacial acetic acid solvent): mix, stir with glass rod, pour into crushed ice/cold water to crystallize, filter, dry. Equation C6H5NH2 + (CH3CO)2O -> C6H5NHCOCH3 + CH3COOH.

Ethyl Alcohol (Prelim final experiment, p84 card) = fermentation of glucose/sucrose with baker's yeast, anaerobic setup with delivery tube into limewater to detect CO2, 2-3 days at 30-35C. Equation C6H12O6 -> 2C2H5OH + 2CO2.

Completion is a two-screen sequence: a 'Mission Complete' banner, then a 'Grade' results panel showing Grade (%), Number of Mistakes (int), Time (MM:SS), with 'Continue' and 'Retry' buttons. Storyboard placeholders show 100% / 0 mistakes / 15:20.

## PharmaSynth Storyboard — Pages 124–164 Digest (Midterm: Acetone & Chloroform; Final: Benzamide start)

### 0. What this page range covers (structure)
These pages storyboard the end of the **Midterm Period** (two full experiments: Acetone, then Chloroform), a top-level **Period-selection screen**, and the beginning of the **Final Period** (Benzamide start). The game is framed as an academic course using Philippine-style grading terms: **Prelim / Midterm / Final "Periods"**, and results are shown as a **"Grade."**

Repeating per-experiment flow (confirmed by page order):
**Menu (pick experiment) → Cutscene title card → Synthesis steps (progress % increases, timer runs) → "Mission Complete" banner → Grade screen (Continue / Retry) → back to Menu.**

Each synthesis step is storyboarded on **two consecutive pages sharing the same progress %** (a wide frame + a duplicate/near-duplicate frame — likely "action" vs "result" framing). The underlying lab background render is often reused across steps; the meaningful change is the reagents/apparatus on the central bench and the progress %.

---

### 1. Navigation / Menu screens

**Period-selection screen (p157)** — `M e n u` header. Rendered as an empty lab corridor with **three grey doors, each with a window** showing a lab inside. Labels: **"Prelim Period", "Midterm Period", "Final Period."** This is the top-level chapter hub; each door = one academic term/chapter of experiments.

**Midterm experiment menu (p124, repeated p140)** — Header text reads **"Midterm Term"** (note: same heading also appears on the Final menu — see open questions). Progress `0%`, timer `00:00:00`, a **Settings** gear (top-left). Background = the main chemistry lab room. The menu lists **4 experiments**, each with name + "…Synthesis" subtitle:
- **Benzoic Acid** — "Benzoic Acid Synthesis"
- **Acetanilide** — "Acetanilide Synthesis"
- **Acetone** — "Acetone Synthesis"
- **Chloroform** — "Chloroform Synthesis"
(Only Acetone and Chloroform are storyboarded within this page range; Benzoic Acid & Acetanilide are listed but detailed elsewhere.) The menu list appears to be presented on an **in-world tablet/iPad device** (grey tablet frame asset with a home button, extracted as an overlay on p124).

**Final experiment menu (p158)** — Header again shows **"Midterm Term"** (label bug/placeholder). Progress `0%`. Lists **4 experiments**:
- **Benzamide** — "Benzamide Synthesis"
- **Aspirin** — "Aspirin Synthesis"
- **Caffeine (from tea)** — "Caffeine Synthesis (from tea)"
- **Wine Making**
Decorative food/drink clip-art icons accompany these items — a high-res **coffee-beans illustration** (Canva clip-art, ~2468×1621) was extracted here, presumably the Caffeine icon (note: coffee beans used for a "from tea" caffeine item — asset mismatch).

---

### 2. Cutscene frames (placeholder)
Cutscenes (p125 Acetone, p141 Chloroform, p159 Benzamide) are **placeholder title cards**: a **blurred version of the lab room** as background with the explicit annotation **"[CUTSCENE: EXPLAINING THE TASK AND THE OBJECTIVE OUTCOME]"** plus the experiment title (e.g., "Synthesis of Acetone", "Synthesis of Chloroform", "Benzamide Synthesis"). No scripted dialogue, no on-screen NPC character, and no storyboarded cutscene action are present — this is a to-be-authored slot for a task/objective explainer.

---

### 3. In-experiment HUD / UI chrome
On every synthesis-step page:
- **Top-left "Settings"** with a **3D gear/cog icon** (rounded, clay-style; extracted as its own asset).
- **"Progress"** label + a **percentage** value.
- A **timer** displayed as `00:00:00` (HH:MM:SS; shows 0 on storyboard, i.e., counts up during play).
- These sit in a HUD panel; the menu uses a **tablet-device frame**, so the progress/settings panel is likely a wrist/tablet-style diegetic UI (exact anchor not annotated).

Progress increments differ per experiment (implying % = completed steps / total steps, with different step counts):
- **Acetone:** 10 → 20 → 40 → 60 → 80 → 100 (uneven; skips 30 and jumps 20→40).
- **Chloroform:** 25 → 50 → 75 → 100 (even quarters; note p147 shows a typo "50" without the % sign).
- **Benzamide:** 20 → 40 → … (20% steps; continues past p164).

---

### 4. Mission Complete + Grade screens (scoring)
**"Mission Complete" banner (p138 Acetone, p155 Chloroform)** overlaid on the finished-experiment bench scene.

**Grade / results screen (p139 Acetone, p156 Chloroform):**
- Header **"Mission Complete."**
- **"Grade"** with a large percentage (100% shown; rendered as big faded number art).
- **"Number of Mistakes"** with a count — **2 for Acetone (p139)**, **0 for Chloroform (p156)**.
- **"Time"** in mm:ss — **15:20** shown on both.
- Action buttons: **"Continue"** and **"Retry."**
This defines the scoring model: **grade % + mistake count + elapsed time**, with retry allowed. (Grade likely derived from mistakes/time, but the exact formula is not specified in the storyboard.)

---

### 5. Player avatar (the "robot")
First-person VR view with **two robot hands**: glossy **white** shells, **dark navy-blue segmented joints/knuckles**, a **glowing purple/indigo ring around each wrist**, and a **circular port/emblem on each palm**. The player IS the robot (no third-person robot NPC is depicted in this range). Hands are shown gripping/pouring reagent bottles (e.g., holding the "DILUTE HCl" bottle on p150), implying **two-handed grab + pour/manipulation** gestures for reagent handling.

---

### 6. Lab environment (reusable scene)
One consistent **chemistry classroom**: perimeter lab benches with **blue cabinets + black/dark countertops**, **wall cabinets with glassware**, a **sink**, **stools**, a **fume/lab area**, **drop ceiling with recessed fluorescent panel lights**, and a **door with a window**. Camera is positioned at a **central island bench** where all experiments are performed. Menu/cutscene screens reuse this room (sharp for menu, blurred for cutscene).

---

### 7. Per-experiment procedures & props

**ACETONE (p126–137)** — Fermentation/distillation setup. Props on bench: beaker of water, an Erlenmeyer flask, an amber **"YEAST"** bottle (also a handwritten-looking "Yudit"/"Yeast" mark), a small amber bottle mislabeled **"DISTILLLD VELET"** (typo), a **distillation flask (Erlenmeyer with tan/amber liquid) sitting in a clear water bath on a black hot-plate/stand**, a **thermometer**, a **condenser/still arm**, a beaker of **blue liquid**, a graduated cylinder, and a large **"DISTILLED WATER"** bottle. The finished/complete scene (p138–139) shows a **ring stand + clamp holding a test tube**, condenser arm, a **bunsen burner with a lit flame**, a **watch glass with white product**, and a clear-liquid beaker. Steps 10–100% reuse the same bench render.

**CHLOROFORM (p142–154)** — Multiple distinct step renders (reagents change per step):
- **p142 (25%):** distillation flask with a white solid/paste sitting in an **ICE BATH (bowl of ice)**, a **"BLEACH"** bottle, a small amber bottle, a graduated beaker, an Erlenmeyer, a glass stir rod, and a condenser arm. (Classic haloform route: bleach/hypochlorite in an ice bath.)
- **p144 (25%):** distillation flask with **purple liquid** in a black heating bath, bottles labeled **"TOLUENE"** and **"POTASSIUM PERMANGANATE"**, a watch glass with white powder, a clear-liquid beaker, ring stand + condenser. (Conflicts with the bleach route — see open questions.)
- **p150 (50%):** one robot hand holding a **"DILUTE HCl"** bottle (pouring), the other holding a small flask of brown liquid; a beaker with **cloudy white precipitate** on the stand; condenser arm. (Acidification/workup step.)
- **p152 (75%):** corked flask on a stand, **white chunks/crystals in an ice-bath beaker**, stir rod, condenser (product crystallization/collection).
- p153–154 (100%) → Mission Complete (p155) → Grade (p156, 0 mistakes, 15:20).

**BENZAMIDE (p159 cutscene, p160–164 steps, continues past 164):**
- **p160 (20%):** flask with **white crystals** on a stand/clamp; a beaker of **yellow liquid on a scale/balance** (left); an Erlenmeyer of clear liquid with a stir rod; **watch glasses/petri dishes with white powder**; a clear-liquid beaker with rod.
- **p162 (20%):** Erlenmeyer with white precipitate + a copper/brown stir rod (flask graduated ~250 mL), watch glasses of white powder, a small flask, an Erlenmeyer of clear liquid, an amber bottle.
- **p164 (40%):** **Erlenmeyer being heated over a bunsen burner (blue flame)** on a stand, a watch glass of white powder, a bottle, a beaker.
(Reagent label text was not clearly legible in this range.)

---

### 8. Notable gaps in this page range
- **No PPE / safety onboarding flow** (goggles, gloves, lab coat donning) is depicted in pages 124–164.
- **No progress "checklist" UI** — progress is a single % + timer, not an itemized step checklist, at least in these pages.
- **No NPC robot dialogue** shown; the robot is the player, and cutscenes are unauthored placeholders.
- **No active timer values** (all 00:00:00); only the Grade screen shows an elapsed time (15:20 placeholder).
- **Wrist/watch UI** is implied via a diegetic tablet frame for the menu, but the exact HUD anchor is not annotated.
